*OrgPath Implementation Series — Part Seven of Eight*

OrgPath and the Microsoft Defender Suite

**Organizational Context in Security Operations**

**Series:** OrgTree and OrgPath — Enterprise Governance Substrate for the Microsoft Identity Stack\
**Document:** Part Seven — Defender Suite Integration Layer\
**Audience:** Senior Security Engineers, SOC Architects, Identity and Access Engineers\
**Prerequisites:** Parts One through Six (Governance Gap, Substrate Requirements, OrgTree/OrgPath Design, Build Implementation, Security Operations Integration, Intune Device Governance)\
**Prepared:** May 2026

<table style="width:1%;">
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 1%" />
</colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><p><strong>⚠ Prerequisites</strong></p>
<p>This document assumes completion of Parts One through Six. The OrgTree registry, OrgPath ETL pipeline, dynamic group library, and the Entra ID extension attribute infrastructure described in those parts are all prerequisites for the integrations documented here. Code samples reference the governance repository structure, registry schema, and naming conventions established in earlier parts.</p></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

Contents

**Chapter 1 — Security Signals Without Organizational Structure**

The context gap in native Defender telemetry

What OrgPath changes about the SOC operating model

**Chapter 2 — Defender for Endpoint: Device Groups and RBAC Scoping**

MDE device group model and OrgPath alignment

Dynamic group-backed MDE device group synchronization

RBAC scoping patterns for organizational SOC roles

**Chapter 3 — Defender for Identity: Investigation Enrichment and Alert Context**

OrgPath in MDI entity profiles

Sentinel analytics rules with organizational differentiation

Surge detection across OrgPath segments

**Chapter 4 — Defender for Cloud Apps: Session Controls and Anomaly Detection**

Conditional Access App Control integration with OrgPath groups

Per-segment anomaly detection thresholds

**Chapter 5 — Defender for Cloud and Defender for Servers: Tag-Based Initiative Targeting**

Azure Policy initiative enforcement by OrgPath tag

Resource Graph queries for organizational security posture

**Chapter 6 — The Unified Defender XDR View: Cross-Product Security Posture by OrgPath**

Unified KQL dashboard query

Sentinel workbook design for organizational SOC operations

**Chapter 7 — Automated Response Playbooks: OrgPath-Aware Incident Containment**

Logic App playbook architecture and response matrix

Tier-differentiated containment automation

**Chapter 8 — The Complete Defender Integration Architecture**

End-to-end data flow from OrgTree to SOC analyst screen

Operational properties delivered by the complete architecture

**CHAPTER ONE**

Security Signals Without Organizational Structure

The Context Gap in Native Defender Telemetry

The Microsoft Defender suite occupies a unique position in the enterprise security toolchain. No other product family generates telemetry of comparable breadth and depth across as many attack surfaces simultaneously. Defender for Endpoint produces continuous behavioral signal from every enrolled workstation and server: process creation chains, network connections, file system writes, registry modifications, memory access patterns, and MITRE ATT&CK technique attributions, all correlated and scored by the cloud-native detection engine. Defender for Identity monitors the authentication fabric itself — Active Directory domain controllers, Entra ID sign-in events, service ticket requests, and lateral movement indicators — producing alert-level detections for techniques that span decades of adversarial tradecraft from Kerberoasting to DCSync. Defender for Cloud Apps watches the application layer: which users are accessing which cloud services, at what volume, from which locations, with what classification of data in scope. Defender for Cloud surveys the infrastructure plane, evaluating every Azure resource and Arc-enrolled server against security baselines and surfacing configuration drift, missing patches, and exposed attack surface. The unified Defender XDR portal and Microsoft Sentinel pull all of these signals into a correlated incident view that, at its best, traces an attack chain across device, identity, application, and infrastructure in a single investigation pane.

The problem is not the volume or quality of the signal. The problem is the absence of a dimension that makes the signal operationally actionable: organizational context. The Defender suite, taken as a whole, knows that a device named DESKTOP-F4A2B1 generated a process injection alert. It knows that a user named jsmith@contoso.com requested a Kerberos service ticket for a highly privileged service account. It knows that a user downloaded eight hundred files from a SharePoint document library in thirty minutes. It knows that an Arc-enrolled server has three critical missing patches and an exposed RDP service. What it does not know — in any form that is systematically surfaced, queryable, or actionable — is where in the organization these events occurred, who is responsible for the assets involved, whether the behavior is anomalous relative to the norms of that organizational segment, and what the correct response procedure is for that organizational context.

The consequences of this gap play out daily in every security operations center that runs the Defender suite without organizational context in the data. An analyst receives a high-severity alert. The alert identifies the device and the technique. To determine the appropriate response, the analyst must answer a sequence of questions that the alert itself cannot answer: Who owns this device? What team does that person work on? What division does that team belong to? What policies govern that division? Is the flagged activity consistent with the normal work patterns of that organizational context? Who is the responsible security contact for this segment of the environment? Which runbook applies? In the absence of a systematic answer to these questions embedded in the alert data itself, the analyst reconstructs the organizational context from memory, from directory lookups, from asset management queries, and from institutional knowledge that may or may not be accurate, current, or consistently applied across the SOC team.

Consider the most direct illustration of why this matters. A penetration test is authorized and running in the engineering division. The penetration testing team is generating exactly the alert signatures that a real attacker would generate: lateral movement, privilege escalation attempts, credential harvesting activity, anomalous SharePoint access. Simultaneously, a real attack is underway in the finance division, executing similar techniques. Both sets of activity appear in the Defender portal with identical alert structures. The engineering alerts are expected, documented, and should be acknowledged and excluded from the active investigation queue. The finance alerts are a live incident requiring immediate containment. Without organizational context in the alert data, differentiating these two scenarios requires out-of-band knowledge — an analyst who happens to know that a pentest is running in engineering, or a process that manually excludes engineering devices from the active incident queue for the pentest window. Neither is systematic. Both fail under load, when analysts are rotating, or when the pentest scope expands without the SOC being notified. The root cause is structural: the alert data carries no organizational position for the affected assets, so the SOC cannot build systematic, data-driven differentiation into its processes.

<table style="width:1%;">
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 1%" />
</colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><p><strong>📝 Organizational Context Is Not Asset Management</strong></p>
<p>It is worth distinguishing between organizational context and asset management metadata. Asset management systems (CMDB, ITAM, SCCM inventory) track device ownership, location, cost center, and lifecycle state. These are useful fields, but they are not the same as organizational position in the governance hierarchy. OrgPath encodes the device's or user's position in the organizational tree — the chain of accountability from the root of the enterprise down to the specific unit responsible for the asset. This is the dimension that drives security policy differentiation, analyst ownership boundaries, and response procedure selection. Asset management metadata may contribute to deriving OrgPath, but it is not a substitute for it.</p></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

What OrgPath Changes About the SOC Operating Model

When every device, user, and Arc-enrolled server in the environment carries an OrgPath value, and when that value is present in the telemetry streams that feed Microsoft Sentinel and the Defender portal, organizational context becomes a first-class attribute in every investigation. The change this makes to the SOC operating model is architectural, not cosmetic.

An alert on a device whose OrgPath is /Root/Finance/Accounting arrives in the analyst's queue already annotated: Finance policies apply, the Accounting unit's responsible SOC analyst is designated by the RBAC role assignment that scopes to the MDE-FINANCE-ACCOUNTING device group, the containment action follows the Finance incident response runbook entry in the response matrix, and the Sentinel workbook places this alert in the Finance organizational view alongside all other Finance-segment alerts from Defender for Identity, Defender for Cloud Apps, and Defender for Cloud. The analyst does not reconstruct any of that context. It is present in the data, surfaced by the platform, and acted on by the automation layer before the analyst even opens the incident.

An alert on a device whose OrgPath is /Root/Engineering/SecurityTesting during a pentest window can be automatically correlated against the pentest authorization record stored in the governance repository, suppressed from the active queue, and tagged as an authorized assessment event — all without analyst intervention — because the OrgPath value in the alert data matches the scope of the authorization record. This is not possible with device name patterns or IP address ranges, both of which are fragile and require constant maintenance. It is possible with OrgPath because OrgPath is derived from the authoritative organizational hierarchy and updated automatically when devices move between organizational units.

The five chapters that follow describe exactly how this organizational context is introduced into each layer of the Defender suite. Chapter Two covers Defender for Endpoint device groups and the RBAC scoping model that gives organizational SOC roles bounded visibility into their assigned segments. Chapter Three covers Defender for Identity investigation enrichment — how OrgPath surfaces in the MDI entity profile and how Sentinel analytics rules fire differentiated alerts based on the organizational position of the affected identity. Chapter Four covers Defender for Cloud Apps session controls and anomaly detection, explaining how per-segment behavioral baselines are established through Conditional Access policy differentiation backed by OrgPath dynamic user groups. Chapter Five covers Defender for Cloud and Defender for Servers, showing how Azure Policy initiative targeting and Resource Graph queries use the OrgPath resource tag on Arc-enrolled servers to produce an infrastructure security posture view organized by organizational hierarchy. Chapter Six presents the unified cross-product KQL query that joins all Defender signal streams into a single organizational security posture dashboard. Chapter Seven presents the Logic App playbook architecture that reads OrgPath at incident creation time and drives contextually appropriate automated response based on the governance-controlled response matrix.

**CHAPTER TWO**

Defender for Endpoint: Device Groups and RBAC Scoping

*Bringing Organizational Structure into the MDE Management Plane*

The MDE Device Group Model and Its Relationship to OrgPath

Defender for Endpoint organizes devices into device groups for two distinct and equally important purposes. The first is policy assignment: device groups are the targeting mechanism for antivirus configuration profiles, attack surface reduction rule sets, endpoint detection and response settings, and automated investigation and remediation configurations. The second is RBAC scoping: device groups determine which analysts and administrators can see and act on which devices in the Defender portal. Both purposes benefit directly from OrgPath, and the integration between them is what makes the organizational SOC model operational at scale.

In the native MDE model, device groups are created manually in the Defender portal, membership rules are configured as tag-based or domain-based filters, and RBAC roles are assigned device group permissions through a separate configuration screen. This model works adequately for small, flat environments. It breaks down as organizational complexity grows: device groups drift out of alignment with the actual organizational structure, tag values require manual maintenance, and analyst scope assignments become inconsistent as teams reorganize. The OrgPath integration replaces the manual maintenance cycle with a systematic, automatically maintained structure derived from the authoritative organizational hierarchy in OrgTree.

The relationship between Entra ID dynamic groups and MDE device groups is the core mechanism of this integration. MDE device groups, configured under **Settings \> Endpoints \> Device groups** in the Defender portal, support Entra ID group-backed membership. When the Entra ID group specified as the membership source is a dynamic group whose membership rule evaluates the OrgPath device extension attribute, the MDE device group membership updates automatically whenever a device's OrgPath changes. The ETL pipeline that writes OrgPath values to device objects — established in Part Four — is the same pipeline that drives this membership. No additional automation is required in MDE itself. The organizational hierarchy, maintained in OrgTree and propagated by the pipeline, governs MDE policy scope and analyst visibility boundaries without any MDE-specific maintenance process.

Device Group Hierarchy Design

Not every OrgTree leaf node warrants its own MDE device group. A team of three developers with identical security policy requirements and a shared SOC analyst does not benefit from a dedicated device group; it adds administrative overhead without adding security differentiation. The design principle is that MDE device groups should be created at the level of organizational granularity at which one or more of the following conditions is true:

- Security policy differentiation exists: the group requires different antivirus settings, ASR rules, or EDR configuration from its sibling groups

- Monitoring sensitivity differs: the group requires a different alert severity threshold, automated investigation scope, or threat intelligence overlay

- Analyst ownership differs: a distinct SOC analyst or team is responsible for this group and should have bounded visibility to it

- Incident response procedure differs: the group's containment and escalation path follows a different runbook

For most organizations, these conditions are met at the division level and the department level. The Finance division has policy requirements that differ from Engineering. The Finance Accounting department has monitoring sensitivity requirements that differ from Finance Operations. Individual teams within Accounting — accounts payable, accounts receivable, payroll — are typically covered by the Accounting department group's branch scope rather than requiring their own device groups. This aligns with the OrgPath branch group model from Part Four: the ORG-BRANCH-FINANCE-ACCOUNTING dynamic group includes all devices in Accounting and all devices in any child node of Accounting, which is exactly the scope appropriate for the Accounting MDE device group.

The following table shows the recommended device group structure for a representative organizational hierarchy. The MDEBoundary flag in the OrgTree registry metadata is the mechanism by which nodes are designated for MDE device group creation:

| **OrgPath** | **Node Type** | **MDEBoundary** | **MDE Device Group** | **Remediation Level** |
|----|----|----|----|----|
| /Root | Root | No | Ungrouped Devices (built-in) | N/A |
| /Root/Infrastructure/Tier0 | Tier | Yes | MDE-INFRA-TIER0 | Full |
| /Root/Infrastructure/Tier1 | Tier | Yes | MDE-INFRA-TIER1 | Partial |
| /Root/Finance | Division | Yes | MDE-FINANCE | Partial |
| /Root/Finance/Accounting | Department | Yes | MDE-FINANCE-ACCOUNTING | Partial |
| /Root/Finance/Treasury | Department | Yes | MDE-FINANCE-TREASURY | Full |
| /Root/Engineering | Division | Yes | MDE-ENGINEERING | NoRemediation |
| /Root/Legal | Division | Yes | MDE-LEGAL | Partial |
| /Root/Executive | Segment | Yes | MDE-EXECUTIVE | Full |

MDE Device Group Synchronization Script

The following PowerShell script reads the OrgTree registry, identifies nodes flagged as MDE governance boundaries, retrieves their associated Entra ID group GUIDs from node metadata, and creates the corresponding MDE device groups via the Defender for Endpoint REST API. The script is idempotent: it checks for existing groups before creating new ones and skips nodes whose MDE group already exists. Run this script whenever the OrgTree registry is updated to add or modify MDE boundary nodes. Authentication uses certificate-based client credentials, which is the recommended approach for unattended automation against the MDE API.

\#Requires -Version 5.1 \<# data-id="167" .SYNOPSIS Creates Defender for Endpoint device groups aligned with OrgPath branch groups. .DESCRIPTION Reads the OrgTree registry for nodes marked as MDE governance boundaries (MDEBoundary: true in node metadata). For each such node, creates an MDE device group backed by the corresponding ORG-BRANCH-{NodeId} Entra ID group. MDE Device Group API: https://api.securitycenter.microsoft.com/api/machinegroups Authentication: OAuth 2.0 client credentials flow using a certificate-bound app registration. Required API permission: WindowsDefenderATP — Machine.ReadWrite.All .PARAMETER OrgTreeRegistryPath Local path to the OrgTree registry JSON file produced by the ETL pipeline. Example: "C:\governance\orgtree\registry.json" .PARAMETER MDETenantId Entra ID tenant ID. Example: "f1e2d3c4-b5a6-f1e2-d3c4-b5a6f1e2d3c4" Substitute your actual tenant GUID from the Entra ID overview blade. .PARAMETER MDEAppId Client ID of the app registration granted WindowsDefenderATP Machine.ReadWrite.All. Example: "a9b8c7d6-e5f4-a9b8-c7d6-e5f4a9b8c7d6" Substitute the Application (client) ID from the app registration overview blade. .PARAMETER MDECertThumbprint Thumbprint of the certificate uploaded to the app registration for client credentials authentication. The certificate must be present in Cert:\LocalMachine\My on the machine running this script. Example: "AABBCCDDEEFF001122334455AABBCCDDEEFF0011" \#\> param( \[string\]\$OrgTreeRegistryPath = "C:\governance\orgtree\registry.json", \[string\]\$MDETenantId = "f1e2d3c4-b5a6-f1e2-d3c4-b5a6f1e2d3c4", \# SUBSTITUTE: Your tenant GUID \[string\]\$MDEAppId = "a9b8c7d6-e5f4-a9b8-c7d6-e5f4a9b8c7d6", \# SUBSTITUTE: Your app registration client ID \[string\]\$MDECertThumbprint = "AABBCCDDEEFF001122334455AABBCCDDEEFF0011" \# SUBSTITUTE: Your certificate thumbprint ) Set-StrictMode -Version Latest \$ErrorActionPreference = "Stop" Write-Host "\[\$(Get-Date -f 'HH:mm:ss')\] Starting MDE device group synchronization" \# --------------------------------------------------------------- \# Step 1: Acquire MDE API Token via Certificate Credential \# The MDE API uses the legacy Azure AD v1 token endpoint (resource=). \# --------------------------------------------------------------- \$cert = Get-Item "Cert:\LocalMachine\My\\MDECertThumbprint" \$tokenUrl = "https://login.microsoftonline.com/\$MDETenantId/oauth2/token" \# Build the JWT client assertion (RFC 7523) \$now = \[DateTimeOffset\]::UtcNow \$header = @{ alg = "RS256"; typ = "JWT"; x5t = \[Convert\]::ToBase64String(\$cert.GetCertHash()) } \| ConvertTo-Json -Compress \$payload = @{ aud = \$tokenUrl exp = \$now.AddMinutes(10).ToUnixTimeSeconds() iss = \$MDEAppId jti = \[Guid\]::NewGuid().ToString() nbf = \$now.ToUnixTimeSeconds() sub = \$MDEAppId } \| ConvertTo-Json -Compress function ConvertTo-Base64Url(\[string\]\$input) { \[Convert\]::ToBase64String( \[Text.Encoding\]::UTF8.GetBytes(\$input) ).TrimEnd('=') -replace '\\', '-' -replace '/', '\_' } \$headerB64 = ConvertTo-Base64Url \$header \$payloadB64 = ConvertTo-Base64Url \$payload \$toSign = "\$headerB64.\$payloadB64" \$rsa = \$cert.GetRSAPrivateKey() \$sigBytes = \$rsa.SignData( \[Text.Encoding\]::UTF8.GetBytes(\$toSign), \[Security.Cryptography.HashAlgorithmName\]::SHA256, \[Security.Cryptography.RSASignaturePadding\]::Pkcs1 ) \$sigB64 = \[Convert\]::ToBase64String(\$sigBytes).TrimEnd('=') -replace '\\', '-' -replace '/', '\_' \$clientAssertion = "\$toSign.\$sigB64" \# Exchange the JWT assertion for an access token \$tokenResponse = Invoke-RestMethod -Method POST -Uri \$tokenUrl -Body @{ grant_type = "client_credentials" client_id = \$MDEAppId client_assertion_type = "urn:ietf:params:oauth:client-assertion-type:jwt-bearer" client_assertion = \$clientAssertion resource = "https://api.securitycenter.microsoft.com" } \$token = \$tokenResponse.access_token \$headers = @{ Authorization = "Bearer \$token" "Content-Type" = "application/json" } Write-Host "\[\$(Get-Date -f 'HH:mm:ss')\] MDE API token acquired" \# --------------------------------------------------------------- \# Step 2: Load OrgTree Registry and Identify MDE Boundary Nodes \# The registry is produced by the ETL pipeline (Part Four). \# Each node with MDEBoundary = true will receive an MDE device group. \# Each such node must also have EntraGroupId populated by the \# dynamic group creation step from Part Four. \# --------------------------------------------------------------- \$registry = Get-Content \$OrgTreeRegistryPath -Raw \| ConvertFrom-Json \$boundaryNodes = \$registry.nodes \| Where-Object { \$\_.Status -eq "Active" -and \$\_.MDEBoundary -eq \$true } Write-Host "\[\$(Get-Date -f 'HH:mm:ss')\] MDE boundary nodes found: \$(\$boundaryNodes.Count)" \# --------------------------------------------------------------- \# Step 3: Get Existing MDE Device Groups (to avoid duplicates) \# --------------------------------------------------------------- \$existingGroups = Invoke-RestMethod \` -Method GET \` -Uri "https://api.securitycenter.microsoft.com/api/machinegroups" \` -Headers \$headers \$existingGroupMap = @{} foreach (\$g in \$existingGroups.value) { \$existingGroupMap\[\$g.name\] = \$g } Write-Host "\[\$(Get-Date -f 'HH:mm:ss')\] Existing MDE device groups: \$(\$existingGroupMap.Count)" \# --------------------------------------------------------------- \# Step 4: Create MDE Device Groups for Each Boundary Node \# --------------------------------------------------------------- foreach (\$node in \$boundaryNodes) { \# MDE device group name is derived from the node ID. \# NAMING CONVENTION: MDE-{NodeId} — adjust prefix to match your environment naming standard. \$mdeName = "MDE-\$(\$node.Id)" if (\$existingGroupMap.ContainsKey(\$mdeName)) { Write-Host " \[SKIP\] Group already exists: \$mdeName" continue } \# Determine automated remediation level from the node's governance tier. \# Tier0 nodes receive Full automated remediation (IR team has pre-approved). \# Tier1 nodes receive Partial (semi-automated, pending analyst approval). \# All other nodes receive NoRemediation (manual analyst action required). \# SUBSTITUTE: Adjust tier-to-remediation mapping for your security posture requirements. \$remediationLevel = switch (\$node.Tier) { "Tier0" { "Full" } "Tier1" { "Partial" } default { "NoRemediation" } } \# EntraGroupId is stored in the OrgTree node metadata, populated when the \# ORG-BRANCH dynamic group was created in Part Four. \# If EntraGroupId is missing, the node was not fully provisioned — skip it. if (\[string\]::IsNullOrEmpty(\$node.EntraGroupId)) { Write-Warning " \[WARN\] Node \$(\$node.Id) has no EntraGroupId — skipping." continue } \$body = @{ name = \$mdeName description = "OrgPath device group: \$(\$node.Name) \| Path: \$(\$node.Path)" isUnassignedMachineGroup = \$false \# This is an explicitly scoped group isAzureActiveDirectoryGroup = \$true \# Membership driven by Entra ID group \# The Entra ID group ID for ORG-BRANCH-{NodeId} — stored in OrgTree node metadata. \# Retrieve separately with: Get-MgGroup -Filter "displayName eq 'ORG-BRANCH-\$(\$node.Id)'" azureActiveDirectoryGroupId = \$node.EntraGroupId remediationLevel = \$remediationLevel } \| ConvertTo-Json -Depth 5 Invoke-RestMethod \` -Method POST \` -Uri "https://api.securitycenter.microsoft.com/api/machinegroups" \` -Headers \$headers \` -Body \$body \| Out-Null Write-Host " \[CREATED\] MDE group: \$mdeName \| Remediation: \$remediationLevel \| EntraGroup: \$(\$node.EntraGroupId)" } Write-Host "\[\$(Get-Date -f 'HH:mm:ss')\] MDE device group synchronization complete."

MDE RBAC Scoping for Organizational SOC Roles

With MDE device groups created and backed by OrgPath-derived dynamic groups, the RBAC layer can be configured to give each organizational SOC team bounded visibility into their assigned segment. MDE custom roles are created under **Settings \> Endpoints \> Roles** in the Defender portal. Each custom role is defined by a set of permissions and a set of device groups to which those permissions apply.

For example, the Finance SOC Analyst role is configured with permissions to view device information, view alerts, manage alert status, and initiate response actions, scoped exclusively to the MDE-FINANCE and MDE-FINANCE-ACCOUNTING device groups. An analyst assigned to this role can see every alert on every Finance device, can manage those alerts, and can initiate isolations and response actions — but cannot see any device, alert, or action in the Engineering, Legal, or Executive device groups. The role assignment is given to the Entra ID security group that represents the Finance SOC team, which may itself be an OrgPath-derived dynamic group if the security operations staff are represented in OrgTree.

This arrangement produces several operational properties that are worth naming explicitly because they are difficult to achieve through other means:

- **Analyst scope automatically follows organizational changes.** When a device moves from one division to another — because a team is reorganized, a project transfers, or a user changes roles — the OrgPath update propagates to the dynamic group, which updates the MDE device group membership, which changes which analyst roles can see the device. No manual RBAC update is required.

- **Analyst scope does not require device naming conventions.** Devices can be named according to any convention without affecting security scope, because scope is derived from OrgPath, not from device names or tags that require administrative maintenance.

- **Scope boundaries are auditable.** Because RBAC scope follows a dynamic group whose membership rule is versioned in the governance repository, any scope change is traceable to a specific OrgTree registry update with an associated pull request and approval record.

<table style="width:1%;">
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 1%" />
</colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><p><strong>ⓘ Ungrouped Device Handling</strong></p>
<p>MDE always maintains a built-in Ungrouped Devices group that captures devices not matched by any custom device group. In an OrgPath-governed environment, ungrouped devices represent assets whose OrgPath has not yet been assigned by the ETL pipeline — newly enrolled devices awaiting their first pipeline run, or devices that failed OrgPath assignment due to a registry gap. The Ungrouped Devices group should be monitored by a dedicated catch-all SOC role, and alerts from ungrouped devices should trigger an automatic OrgPath assignment ticket in the ITSM system. The goal is a zero-ungrouped-device steady state.</p></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

**CHAPTER THREE**

Defender for Identity: Investigation Enrichment and Alert Context

*Making MDI Alerts Organizationally Legible*

What MDI Knows and What It Does Not Know

Defender for Identity is the most identity-focused product in the Defender suite. It deploys sensors on Active Directory domain controllers and reads the Entra ID audit stream to monitor authentication activity, service ticket requests, directory query patterns, and replication traffic. Its detection coverage spans the attack chain that adversaries use once they have an initial foothold in an enterprise environment: reconnaissance through LDAP queries and SPN enumeration, credential access through Kerberoasting and AS-REP roasting, lateral movement through Pass-the-Hash and Pass-the-Ticket, persistence through Golden Ticket and Silver Ticket creation, and privilege escalation through DCSync and AdminSDHolder abuse. Every MDI alert identifies the source account, the target resource or domain controller, the technique with MITRE ATT&CK mapping, the confidence level, and the evidence trail.

What MDI does not natively surface is the organizational position of the affected identities. An analyst investigating a Kerberoasting detection sees the account that requested service tickets and the service accounts that were targeted. To determine the organizational significance of those identities — whether the source is an employee, a contractor, or a service account; which division they belong to; whether the targeted service account is a Tier 0 infrastructure credential or a Tier 2 application service account — the analyst must perform directory lookups outside the MDI investigation screen. This reconstructed context drives the alert severity classification, the escalation decision, and the response priority. Without it, every Kerberoasting detection is treated identically, regardless of whether it targets domain admin credentials or a printer queue service account.

OrgPath in the MDI Entity Profile

When OrgPath is written to the on-premises Active Directory user object as an extension attribute — which it is when the ETL pipeline uses Entra Connect to synchronize OrgPath values back to on-premises AD, as documented in Part Four — MDI displays the extension attribute value in the entity details panel for every user it investigates. The entity profile panel in the MDI investigation experience in the Defender portal shows synchronized directory attributes, and OrgPath appears there alongside department, title, and manager. An analyst opening the source account's entity profile in a Kerberoasting investigation sees the OrgPath value immediately, without performing any additional lookup.

For service accounts, OrgPath placement in the OrgTree is particularly important. Service accounts should be placed in OrgTree nodes that reflect their infrastructure tier — a Tier 0 service account running on a domain controller should carry the path /Root/Infrastructure/Tier0/ServiceAccounts, not an application team path or an unpositioned state. When an MDI alert targets a service account with a Tier 0 OrgPath, the Sentinel analytics rule described below fires a high-severity, high-urgency alert rather than routing the detection to the standard queue. This differentiation is automatic, data-driven, and requires no analyst judgment at intake.

Sentinel Analytics Rule: Sensitive OrgPath Segment MDI Alerts

The following KQL analytics rule is deployed to Microsoft Sentinel. It runs on a five-minute frequency, examines all MDI alerts in the lookback window, joins them to the most recent OrgPath value for each source identity, and fires a high-severity Sentinel incident for any MDI detection involving a principal in a designated sensitive OrgPath branch. The sensitive OrgPath list is maintained as a parameter in the rule and updated when the OrgTree governance boundary classification changes.

// --------------------------------------------------------------- // Sentinel Analytics Rule: MDI Alerts in Sensitive OrgPath Segments // Rule Name: MDI-SensitiveOrgPath-HighSeverity // Frequency: Every 5 minutes // Lookback: 5 minutes // Severity: High // Trigger: Any MDI alert involving a principal in a sensitive OrgPath branch // Incident: Create incident = enabled // Tables: SecurityAlert, AuditLogs // --------------------------------------------------------------- // SUBSTITUTE: Update this list to match the sensitive governance boundaries // in your OrgTree. These prefixes should align with nodes where compromise // would constitute a P1 incident requiring immediate executive notification. let SensitiveOrgPaths = dynamic(\[ "/Root/Executive", "/Root/Infrastructure/Tier0", "/Root/Finance/Treasury", "/Root/Legal", "/Root/Security/SOC" \]); // Pull MDI alerts from the lookback window. // ProviderName for MDI alerts is "Microsoft Defender for Identity". let MDIAlerts = SecurityAlert \| where TimeGenerated \>= ago(5m) \| where ProviderName == "Microsoft Defender for Identity" \| extend // ExtendedProperties carries alert-specific fields as a JSON bag. // "Account Sid" and "User Name" key names are MDI-specific — do not rename. SourceUserId = tostring(todynamic(ExtendedProperties)\["Account Sid"\]), SourceUPN = tostring(todynamic(ExtendedProperties)\["User Name"\]), AlertTechnique = tostring(todynamic(ExtendedProperties)\["Attack tactic"\]) \| project TimeGenerated, SystemAlertId, AlertName, AlertSeverity, SourceUserId, SourceUPN, AlertTechnique, Description; // Build the most recent OrgPath lookup for each user from the Entra ID audit log. // The ETL pipeline writes OrgPath as an "Update user" operation on the extension attribute. // 7-day lookback provides stable coverage even if audit ingestion is intermittent. let UserOrgPaths = AuditLogs \| where TimeGenerated \>= ago(7d) \| where OperationName == "Update user" \| mv-expand Props = AdditionalDetails \| where tostring(Props.displayName) contains "OrgPath" // SUBSTITUTE: Replace "OrgPath" with the exact displayName of your OrgPath // extension attribute as it appears in the Entra ID audit log. // Typically: "extension\_{AppIdWithoutHyphens}\_OrgPath" \| extend UserId = tostring(TargetResources\[0\].id), UPN = tostring(TargetResources\[0\].userPrincipalName), OrgPath = tostring(Props.newValue) \| summarize arg_max(TimeGenerated, OrgPath) by UserId, UPN \| project UserId, UPN, OrgPath; // Join alerts to OrgPath and filter to sensitive segments. MDIAlerts \| join kind=inner UserOrgPaths on \$left.SourceUPN == \$right.UPN \| where OrgPath has_any (SensitiveOrgPaths) \| extend SensitivePath = true, EscalationNeeded = true, // InvestigationTier drives the ITSM queue routing in the Logic App playbook (Chapter 7). InvestigationTier = "P1" \| project TimeGenerated, SystemAlertId, AlertName, AlertSeverity, SourceUPN, OrgPath, AlertTechnique, Description, SensitivePath, EscalationNeeded, InvestigationTier

Sentinel Analytics Rule: MDI Alert Surge Detection by OrgPath Segment

The sensitive segment rule handles the case of a single high-value alert. The surge detection rule handles a different and equally dangerous pattern: a sustained volume of identity-based alerts concentrated in a specific organizational segment. This pattern is characteristic of an adversary who has established persistent access in a division and is conducting ongoing reconnaissance or is attempting multiple techniques in sequence. The individual alerts may each be moderate severity, none triggering the sensitive segment rule on their own, but the concentration of activity in one segment over a rolling hour is a significant compound signal.

// --------------------------------------------------------------- // Sentinel Analytics Rule: MDI Alert Volume Surge by OrgPath Segment // Rule Name: MDI-OrgPath-AlertSurge // Frequency: Every 1 hour // Lookback: 1 hour // Severity: Medium (elevated to High by playbook if trend continues) // Trigger: Any OrgPath segment with \> AlertThreshold alerts in the window // Tables: SecurityAlert, AuditLogs // --------------------------------------------------------------- // SUBSTITUTE: Set AlertThreshold based on your environment's baseline MDI alert // volume per segment per hour during normal operations. // Recommended approach: Run the count query in Log Analytics for 30 days and // set the threshold at 2x the 95th-percentile hourly count per segment. let AlertThreshold = 10; let UserOrgPaths = AuditLogs \| where TimeGenerated \>= ago(7d) \| where OperationName == "Update user" \| mv-expand Props = AdditionalDetails \| where tostring(Props.displayName) contains "OrgPath" // SUBSTITUTE: Match the exact extension attribute displayName in your audit log \| extend UPN = tostring(TargetResources\[0\].userPrincipalName), OrgPath = tostring(Props.newValue) \| summarize arg_max(TimeGenerated, OrgPath) by UPN \| project UPN, OrgPath; SecurityAlert \| where TimeGenerated \>= ago(1h) \| where ProviderName == "Microsoft Defender for Identity" \| extend SourceUPN = tostring(todynamic(ExtendedProperties)\["User Name"\]) \| join kind=leftouter UserOrgPaths on \$left.SourceUPN == \$right.UPN // Devices or accounts without an OrgPath are binned as UNKNOWN. // These should be investigated separately as potential shadow IT or unmanaged accounts. \| extend OrgPath = iif(isempty(OrgPath), "UNKNOWN", OrgPath) \| summarize AlertCount = count(), // make_set captures up to 50 distinct alert types for incident description generation. AlertTypes = make_set(AlertName, 50), AffectedUsers = dcount(SourceUPN) by OrgPath, bin(TimeGenerated, 1h) \| where AlertCount \> AlertThreshold \| extend SurgeDetected = true \| project TimeGenerated, OrgPath, AlertCount, AlertTypes, AffectedUsers, SurgeDetected \| order by AlertCount desc

<table style="width:1%;">
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 1%" />
</colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><p><strong>ⓘ Tuning the Surge Threshold</strong></p>
<p>The AlertThreshold value requires per-environment calibration. Run the surge query without the threshold filter for thirty days of historical data. Calculate the 95th-percentile hourly alert count per OrgPath segment. Set the threshold at twice that value for each segment. If the analytics rule supports parameterized thresholds per segment, create a lookup table in Sentinel that maps OrgPath prefixes to per-segment thresholds. A global threshold will either over-alert on high-activity segments like Infrastructure or under-alert on low-baseline segments like Executive where even five alerts in an hour is a significant anomaly.</p></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

**CHAPTER FOUR**

Defender for Cloud Apps: Session Controls and Anomaly Detection by Organizational Segment

*Scoping Cloud Application Security to Organizational Position*

Why Organizational Context Is Essential for MDCA

Defender for Cloud Apps monitors user behavior across cloud applications and produces anomaly detections when activity deviates from established baselines. The behavioral baseline is the critical variable. MDCA establishes baselines per user and per application, flagging deviations from the user's normal activity pattern. The limitation of this per-user baseline model is that it cannot differentiate between legitimate role-driven behavior and actual anomalies when the user's legitimate behavior is itself atypical relative to the broader tenant baseline.

A data analyst who downloads thousands of records from SharePoint and OneDrive daily as part of their normal workflow will trigger MDCA's bulk download detection if MDCA calibrates its threshold against the tenant-wide average download volume. The analyst is not a threat; the policy is miscalibrated for their organizational context. Conversely, a user in the legal department who downloads fifty documents in a session may fall well below the tenant-wide threshold that triggered the analyst's alert, but fifty documents from a legal matter library is a significant and potentially reportable event in that organizational context. Without organizational context, MDCA cannot differentiate these cases systematically. It either sets a global threshold that under-alerts for data-heavy roles or over-alerts for data-light roles — and neither outcome is operationally sustainable.

OrgPath resolves this by enabling per-segment session policy and anomaly detection configuration. MDCA's session controls are applied through Conditional Access App Control policies, which are Conditional Access policies with session control settings that route application traffic through MDCA's proxy for real-time inspection. When Conditional Access policies are scoped to OrgPath-derived dynamic user groups, the session control parameters — download limits, upload restrictions, file classification filters, session duration controls — are set per organizational segment rather than globally. The data analytics team receives a policy calibrated to their download volume norms. The legal department receives a policy calibrated to their much lower normal activity with correspondingly tighter anomaly thresholds.

Conditional Access App Control Policy Creation by OrgPath Segment

The following PowerShell script creates a Conditional Access policy that applies MDCA session monitoring to a specific OrgPath user group. The script is parameterized so it can be called once per OrgPath node that requires a distinct MDCA session control policy. For nodes that share the same session control parameters, the parent branch group's policy provides coverage through dynamic group inheritance.

\#Requires -Modules Microsoft.Graph.Authentication, Microsoft.Graph.Beta.Identity.SignIns \<# data-id="215" .SYNOPSIS Creates a Conditional Access policy applying MDCA session controls to a specific OrgPath branch user group. .DESCRIPTION Creates a Conditional Access policy in report-only mode that routes sessions for users in the specified OrgPath branch group through Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps for real-time session monitoring. Start all policies in "enabledForReportingButNotEnforced" mode. Validate that no legitimate workflows are blocked by reviewing the sign-in log CA policy report in Entra ID for at least five business days before switching to "enabled". .PARAMETER OrgBranchGroupId Entra ID group GUID of the ORG-BRANCH-{NodeId} user group for this segment. Retrieve with: (Get-MgGroup -Filter "displayName eq 'ORG-BRANCH-{NodeId}'").Id SUBSTITUTE: Replace with the actual GUID for each segment you are onboarding. .PARAMETER NodeId OrgTree node ID for naming and tracking. Must match the node ID in the registry. Example: "FINANCE-ACCOUNTING" .PARAMETER NodeName Human-readable name of the organizational segment. Used in the policy display name. Example: "Finance Accounting" .PARAMETER CloudAppSecurityType MDCA session control type. Options: monitorOnly — Monitor all session activity; no blocking blockDownloads — Allow session but block all file downloads mcasConfigured — Apply the app-specific MDCA policy configured in the MDCA portal SUBSTITUTE: Select appropriate control level per segment sensitivity. Start with monitorOnly for all segments during initial rollout. .NOTES Required Graph scope: Policy.ReadWrite.ConditionalAccess Cloud app IDs are Entra ID service principal appIds for Microsoft cloud apps. 20892 = SharePoint Online, 15600 = OneDrive for Business Add app IDs from the Entra ID Enterprise Applications blade for additional apps. \#\> param( \[Parameter(Mandatory=\$true)\] \[string\]\$OrgBranchGroupId, \[Parameter(Mandatory=\$true)\] \[string\]\$NodeId, \[Parameter(Mandatory=\$true)\] \[string\]\$NodeName, \[ValidateSet("monitorOnly","blockDownloads","mcasConfigured")\] \[string\]\$CloudAppSecurityType = "monitorOnly" ) Connect-MgGraph -Scopes "Policy.ReadWrite.ConditionalAccess" -NoWelcome \$policyBody = @{ displayName = "MDCA Session Control — \$NodeName (ORG-BRANCH-\$NodeId)" \# Start in report-only mode. Switch to "enabled" after validation. \# SUBSTITUTE: Change to "enabled" after reviewing CA sign-in logs. state = "enabledForReportingButNotEnforced" conditions = @{ users = @{ includeGroups = @(\$OrgBranchGroupId) \# SUBSTITUTE: Add exclusion groups for break-glass and service accounts \# that must not be routed through MDCA proxy. excludeGroups = @() } applications = @{ \# 20892 = SharePoint Online, 15600 = OneDrive for Business \# SUBSTITUTE: Add additional Microsoft cloud app IDs as needed. \# Find app IDs in Entra ID \> Enterprise Applications \> the app \> Overview \> Application ID. includeApplications = @("20892", "15600") } \# Apply to all client types — browser and modern auth desktop/mobile clients. clientAppTypes = @("browser", "mobileAppsAndDesktopClients") \# SUBSTITUTE: Add location conditions if the segment has defined work location policies. \# locations = @{ includeLocations = @("AllTrusted"); excludeLocations = @() } } \# No grant controls — this policy applies session controls only. \# Access is governed by separate Conditional Access policies. grantControls = \$null sessionControls = @{ cloudAppSecurity = @{ \# cloudAppSecurityType controls what MDCA does with the routed session. \# monitorOnly: Inspect and log; no enforcement actions. \# blockDownloads: Block all file download attempts. \# mcasConfigured: Apply the per-app MDCA portal policy for this app. cloudAppSecurityType = \$CloudAppSecurityType isEnabled = \$true } \# Re-authentication every 4 hours for sessions under MDCA monitoring. \# SUBSTITUTE: Adjust frequency per segment risk tolerance. signInFrequency = @{ value = 4 type = "hours" authenticationType = "primaryAndSecondaryAuthentication" frequencyInterval = "timeBased" isEnabled = \$true } } } \$result = Invoke-MgGraphRequest \` -Method POST \` -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identity/conditionalAccess/policies" \` -Body (\$policyBody \| ConvertTo-Json -Depth 10) \` -ContentType "application/json" Write-Host "\[SUCCESS\] Created CA policy ID: \$(\$result.id)" Write-Host " Display Name: MDCA Session Control — \$NodeName" Write-Host " State: Report-only (validate before enforcing)" Write-Host " Segment: \$NodeId \| Group: \$OrgBranchGroupId" Disconnect-MgGraph

MDCA Activity Anomaly Detection by OrgPath — Sentinel Workbook Query

The following KQL query is designed for use as a Sentinel workbook tile. It joins the MDCA activity stream from CloudAppEvents to the OrgPath lookup derived from the audit log, producing a per-segment summary of anomaly detections, bulk download events, and sensitive file accesses over the past twenty-four hours. This view gives the SOC an organizational map of cloud application risk that complements the identity risk view from the MDI queries in Chapter Three.

// --------------------------------------------------------------- // MDCA Activity Anomalies by OrgPath // Table: CloudAppEvents (populated when Defender XDR data connector // is enabled in Microsoft Sentinel) // Purpose: Per-segment cloud app anomaly and sensitive access summary // Use: Sentinel workbook tile; set auto-refresh to 1 hour // --------------------------------------------------------------- // Build OrgPath lookup from Entra ID audit log. // SUBSTITUTE: Match the exact displayName of your OrgPath extension attribute. let UserOrgPaths = AuditLogs \| where TimeGenerated \>= ago(7d) \| where OperationName == "Update user" \| mv-expand Props = AdditionalDetails \| where tostring(Props.displayName) contains "OrgPath" \| extend UPN = tostring(TargetResources\[0\].userPrincipalName), OrgPath = tostring(Props.newValue) \| summarize arg_max(TimeGenerated, OrgPath) by UPN \| project UPN, OrgPath; CloudAppEvents \| where TimeGenerated \>= ago(24h) // Filter to events with a resolved account identity. \| where isnotempty(AccountId) // AccountId in CloudAppEvents contains the UPN for Microsoft 365 apps. // SUBSTITUTE: If your environment uses a different account identifier format, // adjust the join key accordingly. For third-party apps, AccountId may be an email address. \| join kind=leftouter UserOrgPaths on \$left.AccountId == \$right.UPN // Users with no OrgPath assignment are flagged as UNPOSITIONED. // UNPOSITIONED activity should be investigated — these may be unmanaged accounts. \| extend OrgPath = iif(isempty(OrgPath), "UNPOSITIONED", OrgPath) \| summarize TotalEvents = count(), // Anomaly events: those flagged by MDCA policy or behavioral detection. // ActionType contains "Anomaly" covers MDCA's built-in anomaly detections. // ActionType contains "Policy" covers custom MDCA policy violations. AnomalyEvents = countif(ActionType contains "Anomaly" or ActionType contains "Policy"), // BulkDownloads: File download events — volume indicates potential data staging. BulkDownloadCount = countif(ActionType == "FileDownloaded"), // SensitiveAccess: Events involving files with sensitivity labels applied. // Sensitivity field is populated when Microsoft Purview labels are in scope. SensitiveAccess = countif(Sensitivity contains "Confidential" or Sensitivity contains "Restricted" or Sensitivity contains "Highly Confidential"), UniqueUsers = dcount(AccountId), UniqueApps = dcount(Application) by OrgPath, bin(TimeGenerated, 1h) // Focus the workbook tile on segments with detected anomalies or elevated sensitive access. // SUBSTITUTE: Adjust thresholds to match your environment's normal sensitivity label volume. \| where AnomalyEvents \> 0 or SensitiveAccess \> 5 \| project TimeGenerated, OrgPath, TotalEvents, AnomalyEvents, BulkDownloadCount, SensitiveAccess, UniqueUsers, UniqueApps \| order by AnomalyEvents desc, SensitiveAccess desc

**CHAPTER FIVE**

Defender for Cloud and Defender for Servers: Tag-Based Initiative Targeting

*Organizational-Context Security Posture for Hybrid Workloads*

The Infrastructure Plane and the OrgPath Resource Tag

The preceding chapters of this document operated primarily on the identity and device planes: users and endpoints. Defender for Cloud operates on a different plane — the infrastructure plane: Azure virtual machines, Arc-enrolled on-premises servers, container workloads, managed databases, and storage accounts. These infrastructure assets carry OrgPath as a resource tag, applied by the tagging pipeline described in Part Five of this series. That resource tag is the bridge between the infrastructure governance model and the Defender for Cloud security posture model, and it enables two capabilities that are not achievable through native Defender for Cloud configuration alone: plan-level differentiation by organizational tier, and security posture reporting organized by organizational hierarchy.

The organizational significance of this is substantial. Enterprise infrastructure is rarely uniform in its security requirements. Tier 0 domain controllers, certificate authorities, and privileged access workstations have security hardening requirements that are categorically more stringent than Tier 1 application servers or Tier 2 developer build machines. Defender for Cloud offers two server protection plans — Plan 1, which includes Microsoft Defender for Endpoint integration and basic vulnerability assessment, and Plan 2, which adds file integrity monitoring, just-in-time VM access, adaptive application controls, Docker host hardening assessment, and the full vulnerability assessment engine. Applying Plan 2 universally across all servers is expensive and may not be operationally warranted for low-sensitivity workloads. Applying Plan 1 uniformly leaves Tier 0 infrastructure without the enhanced detection coverage it requires. OrgPath resource tags enable the organization to express this differentiation through Azure Policy without maintaining manual per-resource or per-subscription plan assignments.

Azure Policy Definition: Tier 0 Defender for Servers Plan 2 Enforcement

The following Azure Policy definition uses the deployIfNotExists effect to automatically remediate Arc-enrolled servers whose OrgPath tag identifies them as Tier 0 infrastructure, ensuring they receive Defender for Servers Plan 2. The policy evaluates the resource tag at every compliance scan cycle. When a server is newly enrolled and its OrgPath tag is applied by the tagging pipeline, the policy's next compliance evaluation will find the server non-compliant and trigger the remediation task automatically.

{ "properties": { "displayName": "OrgPath — Enable Defender for Servers Plan 2 on Tier0 Arc Machines", "description": "Deploys Defender for Servers Plan 2 on Arc-enabled machines whose OrgPath tag identifies them as Tier 0 infrastructure. Uses deployIfNotExists to automatically remediate non-compliant resources. Assign at the management group scope covering all subscriptions that may contain Tier 0 Arc-enrolled servers.", "mode": "Indexed", "parameters": { "OrgPathPrefix": { "type": "String", "defaultValue": "/Root/Infrastructure/Tier0", "metadata": { "displayName": "OrgPath Prefix for Tier 0 Machines", "description": "Arc machines whose OrgPath tag starts with this value receive Defender for Servers Plan 2. SUBSTITUTE: Replace with the exact Tier 0 OrgPath prefix used in your OrgTree registry." } } }, "policyRule": { "if": { "allOf": \[ { // Only evaluate Arc-enrolled machines (on-premises servers connected to Azure). "field": "type", "equals": "Microsoft.HybridCompute/machines" }, { // Match machines whose OrgPath tag contains the Tier 0 prefix. // "contains" is used rather than "startsWith" because Azure Policy // tag condition functions differ from KQL. For exact prefix matching, // use the "like" condition with a trailing wildcard: "/Root/Infrastructure/Tier0\*" "field": "tags\['OrgPath'\]", "contains": "\[parameters('OrgPathPrefix')\]" } \] }, "then": { "effect": "deployIfNotExists", "details": { "type": "Microsoft.Security/defenderForServersSettings", "name": "mma-agent", // Existence check: the resource is compliant if Plan 2 is already enabled. "existenceCondition": { "field": "Microsoft.Security/defenderForServersSettings/plan.name", "equals": "P2" }, // roleDefinitionIds: The managed identity performing remediation requires // the Security Admin role (fb1c8493-542b-48eb-b624-b4c8fea62acd) on the // target subscription. Grant this role to the policy assignment's managed // identity through the Remediation tab of the policy assignment blade. "roleDefinitionIds": \[ "/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleDefinitions/fb1c8493-542b-48eb-b624-b4c8fea62acd" \], "deployment": { "properties": { "mode": "incremental", "template": { "\$schema": "https://schema.management.azure.com/schemas/2019-04-01/deploymentTemplate.json#", "contentVersion": "1.0.0.0", "resources": \[ { "type": "Microsoft.Security/defenderForServersSettings", "apiVersion": "2022-01-01-preview", "name": "mma-agent", "properties": { "plan": { // P2 = Defender for Servers Plan 2 // P1 = Defender for Servers Plan 1 "name": "P2" } } } \] } } } } } } } }

<table style="width:1%;">
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 1%" />
</colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><p><strong>⚠ Policy Assignment Scope</strong></p>
<p>Assign this policy at the management group level that encompasses all subscriptions containing Arc-enrolled servers. Assigning at the subscription level requires creating a separate assignment per subscription and will miss servers in newly created subscriptions until the assignment is manually extended. Management group-level assignment covers all child subscriptions automatically. Ensure the policy assignment's system-assigned managed identity is granted Security Admin at the management group scope, not just the subscription level, to enable cross-subscription remediation tasks.</p></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

Azure Resource Graph Query: Defender for Cloud Findings by OrgPath

Azure Resource Graph provides a query interface across the Azure resource inventory that supports joining resource properties — including tags — with Defender for Cloud assessment results. The following query joins the Arc-enrolled machine inventory with the Defender for Cloud security assessment table, filters to high and critical findings, and aggregates the results by OrgPath to produce a per-segment security posture snapshot. This query is the data source for the infrastructure security posture workbook tile described in Chapter Six, and it is also run by the automation script below to produce the governance repository snapshot for drift detection.

// --------------------------------------------------------------- // Azure Resource Graph: Defender for Cloud Findings by OrgPath // // Run location options: // 1. Azure Portal — Resource Graph Explorer (portal.azure.com) // 2. PowerShell: // Install-Module Az.ResourceGraph // Connect-AzAccount // Search-AzGraph -Query \$queryString // 3. Azure CLI: // az graph query -q "\$queryString" // // Returns: OrgPath segments with unresolved high/critical Defender // for Cloud recommendations on Arc-enrolled servers. // Results ordered by severity for SOC prioritization. // --------------------------------------------------------------- Resources // Filter to Arc-enrolled servers only. // To include Azure VMs, add: or type == "microsoft.compute/virtualmachines" \| where type == "microsoft.hybridcompute/machines" \| extend // SUBSTITUTE: These tag names must match the exact tag key names applied // by your OrgPath tagging pipeline from Part Five. OrgPath = tostring(tags\['OrgPath'\]), OrgNodeId = tostring(tags\['OrgNodeId'\]), OrgTier = tostring(tags\['OrgTier'\]), ResourceId = id // Exclude machines that have not received OrgPath tags yet. \| where isnotempty(OrgPath) \| join kind=inner ( SecurityResources \| where type == "microsoft.security/assessments" // Filter to unresolved findings only. \| where properties.status.code != "Passed" \| extend AssessmentName = tostring(properties.displayName), Severity = tostring(properties.metadata.severity), RemediationDesc = tostring(properties.metadata.remediationDescription), // The assessed resource ID is the full ARM resource ID of the // machine that failed the assessment. AssessedResourceId = tostring(properties.resourceDetails.id) // Focus on High and Critical findings for SOC prioritization. // Remove this filter for a complete compliance view including Medium and Low. \| where Severity in ("High", "Critical") \| project AssessedResourceId, AssessmentName, Severity, RemediationDesc ) on \$left.ResourceId == \$right.AssessedResourceId // Aggregate findings per OrgPath segment. \| summarize CriticalFindings = countif(Severity == "Critical"), HighFindings = countif(Severity == "High"), TotalFindings = count(), // make_set with limit of 10 provides a representative sample of findings. // Remove the limit for a complete finding list (increases result size). FindingSample = make_set(AssessmentName, 10) by OrgPath, OrgTier, OrgNodeId \| order by CriticalFindings desc, HighFindings desc

Security Posture Snapshot Automation Script

The following PowerShell script wraps the Resource Graph query and commits its output to the governance repository as the infrastructure security posture snapshot for the current cycle. This snapshot is consumed by the drift detection process to identify organizational segments where security posture is degrading over time, and it is surfaced in the Sentinel workbook as the infrastructure security posture tile alongside the identity and cloud app risk data from Chapters Three and Four.

\#Requires -Modules Az.ResourceGraph, Az.Accounts \<# data-id="240" .SYNOPSIS Executes the OrgPath-annotated Defender for Cloud assessment query and writes the results to the governance repository for version-controlled posture tracking. .DESCRIPTION Authenticates using a managed identity (for automation server execution), runs the Azure Resource Graph assessment query, and exports the results to the governance repository security posture directory as a timestamped JSON file. This script is intended to run on a schedule (daily or per-shift) as part of the governance automation pipeline. The output file is committed to the governance repository by the CI/CD pipeline after this script completes. .PARAMETER OutputPath Path to write the posture snapshot JSON file. SUBSTITUTE: Set to the path within your governance repository working directory where the CI/CD pipeline will commit the file. Example: "C:\governance\security-posture\defender-cloud-by-orgpath.json" .PARAMETER SubscriptionId Optional. Scope the Resource Graph query to a specific subscription. If omitted, the query runs across all subscriptions in scope for the authenticated identity. SUBSTITUTE: Provide your subscription GUID if needed. .NOTES Connect-AzAccount -Identity authenticates using the managed identity of the compute resource running this script (Azure VM, Arc server, or Azure Automation account). The managed identity must have: - Reader role on all subscriptions containing Arc-enrolled servers - Security Reader role to access SecurityResources in Resource Graph \#\> param( \[string\]\$OutputPath = "C:\governance\security-posture\defender-cloud-by-orgpath.json", \[string\]\$SubscriptionId = "" \# SUBSTITUTE: Your subscription GUID, or leave empty for all ) \$ErrorActionPreference = "Stop" \# Authenticate using managed identity on the automation server. \# SUBSTITUTE: Replace with Connect-AzAccount -ServicePrincipal ... if using \# a service principal instead of a managed identity. Connect-AzAccount -Identity Write-Host "\[\$(Get-Date -f 'HH:mm:ss')\] Running Defender for Cloud OrgPath posture query" \$query = @' Resources \| where type == 'microsoft.hybridcompute/machines' \| extend OrgPath = tostring(tags\['OrgPath'\]), OrgTier = tostring(tags\['OrgTier'\]), OrgNodeId = tostring(tags\['OrgNodeId'\]), ResourceId = id \| where isnotempty(OrgPath) \| join kind=inner ( SecurityResources \| where type == 'microsoft.security/assessments' \| where properties.status.code != 'Passed' \| extend Severity = tostring(properties.metadata.severity), AssessmentName = tostring(properties.displayName), AssessedResourceId = tostring(properties.resourceDetails.id) \| where Severity in ('High', 'Critical') \| project AssessedResourceId, AssessmentName, Severity ) on \$left.ResourceId == \$right.AssessedResourceId \| summarize CriticalFindings = countif(Severity == 'Critical'), HighFindings = countif(Severity == 'High'), TotalFindings = count(), FindingSample = make_set(AssessmentName, 10) by OrgPath, OrgTier, OrgNodeId \| order by CriticalFindings desc '@ \# Build query parameters — scope to subscription if specified \$queryParams = @{ Query = \$query } if (-not \[string\]::IsNullOrEmpty(\$SubscriptionId)) { \$queryParams\["Subscription"\] = @(\$SubscriptionId) Write-Host " Query scope: Subscription \$SubscriptionId" } else { Write-Host " Query scope: All accessible subscriptions" } \$results = Search-AzGraph @queryParams \# Build the output document with provenance metadata \$output = @{ GeneratedAt = (Get-Date).ToUniversalTime().ToString("o") GeneratedBy = "OrgPath-DefenderCloud-PostureSnapshot.ps1" QueryScope = if (\$SubscriptionId) { \$SubscriptionId } else { "All Subscriptions" } TotalSegments = \$results.Count CriticalSegments = (\$results \| Where-Object { \$\_.CriticalFindings -gt 0 }).Count HighSegments = (\$results \| Where-Object { \$\_.HighFindings -gt 0 }).Count Results = \$results } \# Ensure the output directory exists \$outputDir = Split-Path \$OutputPath -Parent if (-not (Test-Path \$outputDir)) { New-Item -ItemType Directory -Path \$outputDir -Force \| Out-Null } \$output \| ConvertTo-Json -Depth 10 \| Set-Content -Path \$OutputPath -Encoding UTF8 Write-Host "\[\$(Get-Date -f 'HH:mm:ss')\] Posture snapshot written to: \$OutputPath" Write-Host " OrgPath segments with findings: \$(\$results.Count)" Write-Host " Segments with Critical findings: \$(\$output.CriticalSegments)" Write-Host " Segments with High findings: \$(\$output.HighSegments)"

**CHAPTER SIX**

The Unified Defender XDR View: Cross-Product Security Posture by OrgPath

*One Pane of Organizational Security Truth*

Why the Unified View Is the Primary Operational Artifact

Chapters Two through Five of this document each addressed one layer of the Defender suite in isolation: device group scoping in MDE, identity alert enrichment in MDI, session control and anomaly detection in MDCA, and infrastructure posture assessment in Defender for Cloud. Each layer produces value on its own. An analyst investigating a specific MDE alert benefits from OrgPath context even if the other layers are not yet integrated. An SOC manager reviewing the Defender for Cloud posture snapshot organized by OrgPath gets organizational clarity even without the identity layer connected. But the full operational payoff of the OrgPath model comes from the unified view that combines all of these signals into a single organizational security posture picture.

The unified Sentinel workbook organized by OrgPath hierarchy is the primary operational artifact for an SOC manager in an OrgPath-governed environment. It answers the question that every security leader asks but that conventional tooling cannot answer concisely: Which parts of the organization are under the most security pressure right now, across all dimensions? The conventional answer to this question requires the manager to aggregate data from four or more dashboards, apply their own judgment about which signals are most significant, and mentally map device names and user accounts to organizational structures. The OrgPath-organized workbook answers this question directly and continuously, with the organizational hierarchy as the primary axis of the entire view.

Data Source Requirements

The unified cross-product KQL query joins data from the following tables in the Microsoft Sentinel workspace. All tables must be populated for the unified view to be complete:

| **Table** | **Source** | **Required For** | **Sentinel Connector** |
|----|----|----|----|
| SecurityAlert | Microsoft Defender XDR | MDE and MDI alert counts | Microsoft Defender XDR |
| CloudAppEvents | Microsoft Defender XDR | MDCA session and anomaly data | Microsoft Defender XDR |
| AuditLogs | Entra ID | OrgPath lookup (user and device) | Azure Active Directory |
| DeviceInfo | Microsoft Defender XDR | Device name to OrgPath mapping | Microsoft Defender XDR |

Unified Cross-Product Sentinel Dashboard Query

The following KQL query is saved as a Sentinel workbook query with a one-hour auto-refresh interval. It produces a table of OrgPath segments with aggregated signal counts from MDE, MDI, and MDCA for the past twenty-four hours, ordered by total signal volume. This table is the primary organizational security posture view for the SOC operations center screen.

// --------------------------------------------------------------- // Unified OrgPath Security Posture Dashboard — Cross-Product View // // Purpose: Single pane showing alert and anomaly distribution across // all Defender products, organized by organizational segment. // Refresh: 1 hour (configure in Sentinel workbook tile settings) // Tables: SecurityAlert, CloudAppEvents, AuditLogs // // Configure this as a Sentinel Workbook grid tile with: // - Column sparklines for MDEAlertCount and MDIAlertCount // - Threshold-based row coloring: red if TotalSignals \> 20, // amber if \> 5, green otherwise // - OrgPath column as the drill-down link to the segment workbook // --------------------------------------------------------------- // --- OrgPath Lookup: Most Recent Path per User --- // materialize() caches the result for reuse across multiple joins // without re-executing the subquery, which improves performance significantly // on large AuditLogs tables. let UserOrgPaths = materialize( AuditLogs \| where TimeGenerated \>= ago(7d) \| where OperationName == "Update user" \| mv-expand Props = AdditionalDetails // SUBSTITUTE: Replace "OrgPath" with the exact displayName of your extension attribute \| where tostring(Props.displayName) contains "OrgPath" \| extend UPN = tostring(TargetResources\[0\].userPrincipalName), OrgPath = tostring(Props.newValue) \| summarize arg_max(TimeGenerated, OrgPath) by UPN \| project UPN, OrgPath ); // --- OrgPath Lookup: Most Recent Path per Device --- let DeviceOrgPaths = materialize( AuditLogs \| where TimeGenerated \>= ago(7d) \| where OperationName == "Update device" \| mv-expand Props = AdditionalDetails \| where tostring(Props.displayName) contains "OrgPath" \| extend // DeviceName in the audit log matches the displayName of the device object. // Confirm this matches how MDE surfaces device names in ExtendedProperties. DeviceName = tostring(TargetResources\[0\].displayName), OrgPath = tostring(Props.newValue) \| summarize arg_max(TimeGenerated, OrgPath) by DeviceName \| project DeviceName, OrgPath ); // --- Metric 1: MDE Alert Summary by OrgPath Segment --- let MDEAlerts = SecurityAlert \| where TimeGenerated \>= ago(24h) \| where ProviderName == "Microsoft Defender for Endpoint" \| extend // "Machine Name" is the ExtendedProperties key for the device name in MDE alerts. DeviceName = tostring(todynamic(ExtendedProperties)\["Machine Name"\]) \| join kind=leftouter DeviceOrgPaths on DeviceName \| extend OrgPath = iif(isempty(OrgPath), "UNPOSITIONED", OrgPath) \| summarize MDEAlertCount = count(), // High and Critical severity counts drive the threshold-based row coloring. MDEHighSeverity = countif(AlertSeverity in ("High", "Critical")) by OrgPath; // --- Metric 2: MDI Alert Summary by OrgPath Segment --- let MDIAlerts = SecurityAlert \| where TimeGenerated \>= ago(24h) \| where ProviderName == "Microsoft Defender for Identity" \| extend // "User Name" is the ExtendedProperties key for the source UPN in MDI alerts. SourceUPN = tostring(todynamic(ExtendedProperties)\["User Name"\]) \| join kind=leftouter UserOrgPaths on \$left.SourceUPN == \$right.UPN \| extend OrgPath = iif(isempty(OrgPath), "UNPOSITIONED", OrgPath) \| summarize MDIAlertCount = count(), MDIHighSeverity = countif(AlertSeverity in ("High", "Critical")) by OrgPath; // --- Metric 3: MDCA Anomalies by OrgPath Segment --- let MDCAEvents = CloudAppEvents \| where TimeGenerated \>= ago(24h) // MDCA anomaly and policy violation events carry "Anomaly" or "Policy" in ActionType. // SUBSTITUTE: Review your CloudAppEvents ActionType values and adjust the filter // to match the specific anomaly event types relevant to your MDCA policy set. \| where ActionType contains "Anomaly" or ActionType contains "Policy" \| join kind=leftouter UserOrgPaths on \$left.AccountId == \$right.UPN \| extend OrgPath = iif(isempty(OrgPath), "UNPOSITIONED", OrgPath) \| summarize MDCAAnomolies = count() by OrgPath; // --- Unified Join: Combine All Three Metric Streams --- // fullouter join ensures all OrgPath segments appear in the result // even if they have activity in only one of the three products. MDEAlerts \| join kind=fullouter MDIAlerts on OrgPath \| join kind=fullouter MDCAEvents on OrgPath // coalesce resolves OrgPath from whichever join arm is non-null. \| extend OrgPath = coalesce(OrgPath, OrgPath1, OrgPath2, "UNKNOWN") // Replace nulls from outer join with zeros for clean display. \| extend MDEAlertCount = coalesce(MDEAlertCount, 0), MDEHighSeverity = coalesce(MDEHighSeverity, 0), MDIAlertCount = coalesce(MDIAlertCount, 0), MDIHighSeverity = coalesce(MDIHighSeverity, 0), MDCAAnomolies = coalesce(MDCAAnomolies, 0) \| extend TotalSignals = MDEAlertCount + MDIAlertCount + MDCAAnomolies \| project OrgPath, MDEAlertCount, MDEHighSeverity, MDIAlertCount, MDIHighSeverity, MDCAAnomolies, TotalSignals \| order by TotalSignals desc

Sentinel Workbook Design for Organizational SOC Operations

The unified query above serves as the primary grid tile in a Sentinel workbook configured with the following layout. The workbook is organized top-to-bottom to answer progressively more specific questions as the analyst drills down:

- **Row 1 — Executive Summary Tiles:** Four metric tiles showing total MDE alerts, total MDI alerts, total MDCA anomalies, and count of organizational segments with any High or Critical severity signal in the past twenty-four hours. These tiles are visible on the operations center display without scrolling.

- **Row 2 — OrgPath Hierarchy Grid:** The unified query result table, with OrgPath as a clickable column that filters all subsequent tiles in the workbook to the selected segment. Row colors are threshold-driven: red for TotalSignals greater than twenty, amber for greater than five, green otherwise. This threshold should be calibrated per segment as organizational baselines are established.

- **Row 3 — Segment Detail (conditionally rendered on OrgPath selection):** Four sub-tiles showing the alert time series for MDE, MDI alert type breakdown, MDCA anomaly detail table, and the Defender for Cloud infrastructure finding count from the posture snapshot. This row is hidden until an analyst selects a specific OrgPath segment from the grid above.

- **Row 4 — Unpositioned Assets:** A dedicated tile showing devices and users with no OrgPath assignment, their alert counts, and their last seen dates. This tile represents governance debt: every asset in this view should receive an OrgPath assignment through the pipeline before the next reporting cycle.

**CHAPTER SEVEN**

Automated Response Playbooks: OrgPath-Aware Incident Containment

*Playbooks That Know Where the Incident Is*

Generic Versus Organizational Incident Response Automation

Sentinel automation rules and Logic App playbooks have been available to SOC teams since Sentinel's general availability, and most mature SOC operations have some playbook automation in place. The typical playbook triggered by a high-severity Sentinel incident sends a notification to a Teams channel or security distribution list, creates an incident ticket in the ITSM system, optionally isolates the affected device in MDE, and closes the automation run. Every high-severity incident receives the same treatment: the same notification channel, the same ITSM queue, the same isolation decision. The playbook is alert-class-aware — it might behave differently for a ransomware detection than for an impossible travel alert — but it is not organization-aware. It cannot differentiate between a high-severity alert on a Tier 0 domain controller and the same alert type on a Tier 2 developer workstation in terms of escalation path, notification list, containment urgency, or review requirements.

An OrgPath-aware playbook changes this. When the playbook reads the OrgPath of the affected identity or device as its first action after receiving the incident trigger, everything that follows can be calibrated to the organizational context. The escalation contact is drawn from the segment's designated SOC team, not a global distribution list. The ITSM queue is the Finance SOC queue or the Infrastructure Tier 0 queue, not a single undifferentiated security queue. The isolation decision is automatic for Tier 0 assets but requires analyst approval for Tier 2 assets because the operational impact of isolating a developer workstation is very different from isolating a domain controller. The access review trigger fires immediately for any incident involving an Executive-segment identity but enters a standard review cycle for a Tier 2 incident. These are not differences in degree; they are differences in kind, and they can only be expressed systematically when the playbook knows where in the organization the incident occurred.

The Response Matrix: Governance-Controlled Playbook Configuration

The playbook's organizational intelligence is not hardcoded in the Logic App definition. It is stored in a JSON file in the governance repository at governance/security/response-matrix.json and retrieved by the playbook at runtime via the repository's REST API. This separation is essential: it means that changes to response procedures for any organizational segment — a change in the Finance escalation contact, a decision to enable automatic isolation for a new department, an update to the Tier 0 PIM revocation policy — are made through the same governed pull request, review, and approval process used for all other governance artifacts. The playbook code does not change; the governance document changes, and the playbook picks up the new configuration on its next execution.

The response matrix structure is illustrated below for three representative OrgPath segments:

// governance/security/response-matrix.json // --------------------------------------------------------------- // OrgPath Response Matrix // Format: Array of response configurations ordered from most specific // to least specific OrgPath prefix. The playbook evaluates // prefixes in array order and applies the first match. // More specific paths (longer strings) must appear before // their parent paths in the array. // // Fields: // orgPathPrefix — OrgPath prefix this entry applies to // escalationGroupId — Entra ID group GUID for escalation notification // itsmQueue — ITSM queue name for ticket creation // autoIsolate — true: isolate device automatically // false: require analyst approval // revokePIM — true: suspend PIM eligible assignments pending review // triggerAccessReview — true: create an access review for the affected user // responseRunbook — URL to the applicable runbook in the team wiki // incidentPriority — P1/P2/P3 classification for ITSM routing // --------------------------------------------------------------- { "version": "1.0", "lastUpdated": "2026-05-08", "approvedBy": "Security Architecture Board", "configurations": \[ { "orgPathPrefix": "/Root/Infrastructure/Tier0", "escalationGroupId": "aaaaaaaa-1111-aaaa-1111-aaaaaaaaaaaa", "itsmQueue": "SEC-TIER0-CRITICAL", "autoIsolate": true, "revokePIM": true, "triggerAccessReview": true, "responseRunbook": "https://wiki.internal/security/runbooks/tier0-incident", "incidentPriority": "P1" }, { "orgPathPrefix": "/Root/Finance/Treasury", "escalationGroupId": "bbbbbbbb-2222-bbbb-2222-bbbbbbbbbbbb", "itsmQueue": "SEC-FINANCE-P1", "autoIsolate": true, "revokePIM": true, "triggerAccessReview": true, "responseRunbook": "https://wiki.internal/security/runbooks/finance-treasury", "incidentPriority": "P1" }, { "orgPathPrefix": "/Root/Finance", "escalationGroupId": "cccccccc-3333-cccc-3333-cccccccccccc", "itsmQueue": "SEC-FINANCE-STANDARD", "autoIsolate": false, "revokePIM": false, "triggerAccessReview": true, "responseRunbook": "https://wiki.internal/security/runbooks/finance-standard", "incidentPriority": "P2" }, { "orgPathPrefix": "/Root/Executive", "escalationGroupId": "dddddddd-4444-dddd-4444-dddddddddddd", "itsmQueue": "SEC-EXECUTIVE-VIP", "autoIsolate": false, "revokePIM": true, "triggerAccessReview": true, "responseRunbook": "https://wiki.internal/security/runbooks/executive-incident", "incidentPriority": "P1" }, { "orgPathPrefix": "/Root", "escalationGroupId": "eeeeeeee-5555-eeee-5555-eeeeeeeeeeee", "itsmQueue": "SEC-STANDARD", "autoIsolate": false, "revokePIM": false, "triggerAccessReview": false, "responseRunbook": "https://wiki.internal/security/runbooks/standard-incident", "incidentPriority": "P3" } \] }

Logic App Playbook: OrgPath-Aware Incident Response

The following Logic App definition implements the OrgPath-aware incident response playbook. The playbook is triggered by a Sentinel incident creation event.
